body:
## Summary

The d-server builds its tonic gRPC server with **all defaults** — no concurrency limit, no request timeout, no HTTP/2 or TCP tuning. So it has **no backpressure / fail-closed behaviour** under overload (contrary to architecture §8.9), and per-connection request parallelism is left at the h2 default. Configure the server for controlled concurrency.

## Root cause

`DServer::serve` constructs the server as a bare `Server::builder().add_service(...)` (`crates/server/src/dserver.rs:160`), setting none of:

- `concurrency_limit_per_connection` / a global concurrency limit
- a request `timeout`
- `max_concurrent_streams` (HTTP/2 inbound stream cap)
- `tcp_nodelay`, HTTP/2 keepalive

## Impact

- **No fail-closed under pressure.** Unbounded concurrent requests all contend for runtime threads; combined with blocking storage I/O (companion issue) an overload spills into **thread-pool exhaustion instead of a clean, retryable "busy" signal**. This is the opposite of architecture §8.9 ("the system fails closed under pressure… shed or slow load predictably… never trade correctness for admission", `docs/design/architecture/08-crosscutting-concepts.md:98-107`).
- **Per-connection parallelism left at the default.** A single client channel multiplexes only up to the h2 default concurrent-stream cap per connection (the exact default should be confirmed against the pinned `tonic`/`h2` version rather than assumed); a storage workload may want it raised. Multiple connections multiply it, but the per-connection ceiling is currently implicit.
- **Small-RPC latency** may suffer without `tcp_nodelay` (Nagle) — lower confidence; confirm tonic's default before changing.

## Fix (the configuration knobs)

Configure the tonic `Server` for *controlled* parallelism and backpressure:

- **`concurrency_limit`** (global and/or per-connection) so the d-server sheds with a retryable status under overload instead of exhausting threads (fail-closed, §8.9). A tower load-shed / timeout layer is the alternative.
- **request `timeout`** so a stuck handler can't pin a slot indefinitely.
- **`max_concurrent_streams`** tuned for the storage workload (after confirming the current default).
- **`tcp_nodelay`** and **HTTP/2 keepalive** for latency/liveness — confirm defaults first.

### Caveat — match the device, make it tunable

The concurrency limit should track the storage device's useful queue depth: unbounded parallel random I/O on a single HDD causes seek storms; NVMe benefits from deeper queues. Expose the limit (and the companion issue's blocking-pool size) as **config with HDD-vs-SSD-appropriate defaults**, not a hardcoded constant.

## Definition of done

- [ ] the d-server sets an explicit concurrency limit (or load-shed layer) and a request timeout — overload returns a retryable status, not thread-pool exhaustion
- [ ] `max_concurrent_streams` / `tcp_nodelay` / keepalive set explicitly (after confirming current defaults), or a comment recording why the default is kept
- [ ] the limits are configurable, with documented HDD/SSD-appropriate defaults
- [ ] a load test shows graceful shedding under overload (fail-closed per §8.9)

## Refs

- `crates/server/src/dserver.rs:160` (`Server::builder()`, all defaults)
- `docs/design/architecture/08-crosscutting-concepts.md:98-107` (§8.9 admission control / fail-closed)
- Companion: FsChunkStore blocking-I/O offload issue (backpressure matters most once I/O is off the reactor). Related: #203, ADR-0032.

milestone.title: M3 — Custodians
milestone.description: The self-maintaining durability plane (L4): GC, scrub, reconstruction, rebalance + durability telemetry. Makes single-zone trustworthy; gates the Step-2 release. Plan: proposal 0005.

title: d-server: gRPC server sets no concurrency limit, backpressure, or HTTP/2 tuning (all tonic defaults)
